explanation: The phrase "shall not permit" indicates a global constraint that the condition `prop_0` must never be true. Therefore, the LTL formula expresses that it is Always (Globally) Not `prop_0`.

The phrase "shall not permit" indicates that the proposition `prop_0` must never be true. In LTL, "never" or "globally not" is represented by the `G` (globally) operator combined with the `!` (negation) operator. Thus, `G !prop_0` means that it is always the case that `prop_0` is false.
The phrase "shall not permit" indicates that the action represented by 'prop_0' must never occur. This translates directly to a global negation of 'prop_0'.

The phrase "shall not permit" indicates that the proposition should never be true at any point in time. In LTL, this is expressed using the 'Globally' (G) operator combined with negation (!). So, 'G !prop_0' means that it is always the case that prop_0 is false.

The phrase "shall not permit" indicates that something must never happen. In LTL, "never" is expressed by the Global operator (G) combined with negation (!). Therefore, "The system shall not permit prop_0" translates to "Globally not prop_0".
The phrase "shall not permit prop_0" means that prop_0 must never be true at any point in time. This translates to "Globally not prop_0".

The phrase "shall not permit X to Y" translates to "it is never the case that Y is true". In LTL, "never Y" is expressed as G !Y, meaning "globally not Y". Therefore, "not permit prop_0" becomes G !prop_0.

The phrase "shall not permit" means that `prop_0` must always be false in all future states, including the current state. This translates directly to the Global operator (G) applied to the negation of `prop_0`.
The phrase "The system shall not permit prop_0" means that prop_0 must never be true at any point in the future. In LTL, "never" is expressed using the 'G' (globally) operator combined with negation.

The statement "The system shall not permit... to prop_0" implies that 'prop_0' should never occur. In LTL, 'never' is expressed by the 'globally not' operator (G !). Therefore, the translation is 'G !prop_0'.
